is a [bat-and-ball game](/wiki/Bat-and-ball_games "Bat-and-ball games") that is played between two [teams](/wiki/Sports_team "Sports team") of eleven players on a [field](/wiki/Cricket_field "Cricket field"), at the centre of which is a 22-yard (20-metre; 66-foot) [pitch](/wiki/Cricket_pitch "Cricket pitch") with a [wicket](/wiki/Wicket "Wicket") at each end, each comprising two [bails](/wiki/Bail_(cricket) "Bail (cricket)") (small sticks) balanced on three [stumps](/wiki/Stump_(cricket) "Stump (cricket)"). Two players from the [batting](/wiki/Batting_(cricket) "Batting (cricket)") team, the striker and nonstriker, stand in front of either wicket holding [bats](/wiki/Cricket_bat "Cricket bat"), while one player from the [fielding](/wiki/Fielding_(cricket) "Fielding (cricket)") team, the bowler, [bowls](/wiki/Bowling_(cricket) "Bowling (cricket)") the [ball](/wiki/Cricket_ball "Cricket ball") toward the striker's wicket from the opposite end of the pitch. The striker's goal is to hit the bowled ball with the bat and then switch places with the [nonstriker](/wiki/Nonstriker "Nonstriker"), with the batting team scoring one [run](/wiki/Run_(cricket) "Run (cricket)") for each of these swaps. Runs are also scored when the ball reaches the [boundary](/wiki/Boundary_(cricket) "Boundary (cricket)") of the field or when the ball is bowled [illegally](/wiki/Illegal_delivery_(cricket) "Illegal delivery (cricket)").

**Cricket** is a [bat-and-ball game](/wiki/Bat-and-ball_games "Bat-and-ball games") that is played between two [teams](/wiki/Sports_team "Sports team") of eleven players on a [field](/wiki/Cricket_field "Cricket field"), at the centre of which is a 22-yard (20-metre; 66-foot) [pitch](/wiki/Cricket_pitch "Cricket pitch") with a [wicket](/wiki/Wicket "Wicket") at each end, each comprising two [bails](/wiki/Bail_(cricket) "Bail (cricket)") (small sticks) balanced on three [stumps](/wiki/Stump_(cricket) "Stump (cricket)"). Two players from the [batting](/wiki/Batting_(cricket) "Batting (cricket)") team, the striker and nonstriker, stand in front of either wicket holding [bats](/wiki/Cricket_bat "Cricket bat"), while one player from the [fielding](/wiki/Fielding_(cricket) "Fielding (cricket)") team, the bowler, [bowls](/wiki/Bowling_(cricket) "Bowling (cricket)") the [ball](/wiki/Cricket_ball "Cricket ball") toward the striker's wicket from the opposite end of the pitch. The striker's goal is to hit the bowled ball with the bat and then switch places with the [nonstriker](/wiki/Nonstriker "Nonstriker"), with the batting team scoring one [run](/wiki/Run_(cricket) "Run (cricket)") for each of these swaps. Runs are also scored when the ball reaches the [boundary](/wiki/Boundary_(cricket) "Boundary (cricket)") of the field or when the ball is bowled [illegally](/wiki/Illegal_delivery_(cricket) "Illegal delivery (cricket)").

The fielding team aims to prevent runs by [dismissing](/wiki/Dismissal_(cricket) "Dismissal (cricket)") batters (so they are "out"). Dismissal can occur in various ways, including being [bowled](/wiki/Bowled "Bowled") (when the ball hits the striker's wicket and dislodges the bails), and by the fielding side either [catching](/wiki/Caught "Caught") the ball after it is hit by the bat but before it hits the ground, or hitting a wicket with the ball [before](/wiki/Run_out "Run out") a batter can cross the [crease line](/wiki/Crease_(cricket) "Crease (cricket)") in front of the wicket. When ten batters have been dismissed, the [innings](/wiki/Innings "Innings") (playing phase) ends and the teams swap roles. [Forms of cricket](/wiki/Forms_of_cricket "Forms of cricket") range from traditional [Test matches](/wiki/Test_cricket "Test cricket") played over five days to the newer [Twenty20](/wiki/Twenty20 "Twenty20") format (also known as *T20*), in which each team bats for a single innings of 20 [overs](/wiki/Over_(cricket) "Over (cricket)") (each "over" being a set of 6 fair opportunities for the batting team to score) and the game generally lasts three to four hours.

Traditionally, cricketers play in all-white [kit](/wiki/Cricket_clothing_and_equipment "Cricket clothing and equipment"), but in [limited overs cricket](/wiki/Limited_overs_cricket "Limited overs cricket"), they wear club or team colours. In addition to the basic kit, some players wear protective gear to prevent injury caused by the ball, which is a hard, solid spheroid made of compressed [leather](/wiki/Leather "Leather") with a slightly raised sewn seam enclosing a [cork](/wiki/Cork_(material) "Cork (material)") core layered with tightly wound string.

The earliest known definite reference to cricket is to it being played in [South East England](/wiki/South_East_England "South East England") in the mid-16th century. It spread globally with the expansion of the [British Empire](/wiki/British_Empire "British Empire"), with the first international matches in the second half of the 19th century. The game's governing body is the [International Cricket Council](/wiki/International_Cricket_Council "International Cricket Council") (ICC), which has over 100 members, twelve of which are [full members](/wiki/List_of_International_Cricket_Council_members "List of International Cricket Council members") who play Test matches. The game's rules, the *[Laws of Cricket](/wiki/Laws_of_Cricket "Laws of Cricket")*, are maintained by [Marylebone Cricket Club](/wiki/Marylebone_Cricket_Club "Marylebone Cricket Club") (MCC) in [London](/wiki/London "London"). The sport is primarily played in [India](/wiki/India "India"), [Pakistan](/wiki/Pakistan "Pakistan"), [Bangladesh](/wiki/Bangladesh "Bangladesh"), [Sri Lanka](/wiki/Sri_Lanka "Sri Lanka"), [Afghanistan](/wiki/Afghanistan "Afghanistan"), [Australia](/wiki/Australia "Australia"), [New Zealand](/wiki/New_Zealand "New Zealand"), [England](/wiki/England "England") and [Wales](/wiki/Wales "Wales"), [South Africa](/wiki/South_Africa "South Africa") and the [West Indies](/wiki/West_Indies "West Indies").[[2]](#cite_note-:0-2)

While cricket has traditionally been played largely by men, [women's cricket](/wiki/Women%27s_cricket "Women's cricket") has experienced significant growth in the 21st century.[[3]](#cite_note-3)

The most successful side playing [international cricket](/wiki/International_cricket "International cricket") is [Australia](/wiki/Australia_national_cricket_team "Australia national cricket team"), which has won eight [One Day International](/wiki/One_Day_International "One Day International") trophies, including six [World Cups](/wiki/Cricket_World_Cup "Cricket World Cup"), more than any other country, and has been the [top-rated Test side](/wiki/ICC_Men%27s_Test_Team_Rankings "ICC Men's Test Team Rankings") more than any other country.[[4]](#cite_note-4)[[5]](#cite_note-5)

## History

Main article: [History of cricket](/wiki/History_of_cricket "History of cricket")

### Origins

Main article: [History of cricket to 1725](/wiki/History_of_cricket_to_1725 "History of cricket to 1725")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/38/Cantigas_de_Santa_Mar%C3%ADa_-_Xogo_da_pelota.jpg/250px-Cantigas_de_Santa_Mar%C3%ADa_-_Xogo_da_pelota.jpg)](/wiki/File:Cantigas_de_Santa_Mar%C3%ADa_-_Xogo_da_pelota.jpg)

A medieval "club ball" game involving an underarm bowl towards a batter. Ball catchers are shown positioning themselves to catch a ball. Detail from the *[Canticles of Holy Mary](/wiki/Cantigas_de_Santa_Maria "Cantigas de Santa Maria")*, 13th century.

Cricket is one of many games in the "club ball" sphere that involve hitting a ball with a hand-held implement. Others include [baseball](/wiki/Baseball "Baseball") (which shares many [similarities](/wiki/Comparison_of_baseball_and_cricket "Comparison of baseball and cricket") with cricket, both belonging in the more specific [bat-and-ball games](/wiki/Bat-and-ball_games "Bat-and-ball games") category[[6]](#cite_note-6)), [golf](/wiki/Golf "Golf"), [hockey](/wiki/Hockey "Hockey"), [tennis](/wiki/Tennis "Tennis"), [squash](/wiki/Squash_(sport) "Squash (sport)"), [badminton](/wiki/Badminton "Badminton") and [table tennis](/wiki/Table_tennis "Table tennis").[[7]](#cite_note-JM17-7) In cricket's case, a key difference is the existence of a solid target structure, the wicket (originally, it is thought, a "[wicket gate](/wiki/Wicket_gate "Wicket gate")" through which sheep were herded), that the batter must defend.[[8]](#cite_note-BWC1-8) The cricket historian [Harry Altham](/wiki/Harry_Altham "Harry Altham") identified three "groups" of "club ball" games: the "hockey group", in which the ball is driven to and from between two targets (the goals); the "golf group", in which the ball is driven towards an undefended target (the hole); and the "cricket group", in which "the ball is aimed at a mark (the wicket) and driven away from it".[[9]](#cite_note-HSA19-9)

It is generally believed that cricket originated as a [children's game](/wiki/Children%27s_game "Children's game") in the south-eastern counties of England, sometime during the [medieval period](/wiki/England_in_the_Middle_Ages "England in the Middle Ages").[[8]](#cite_note-BWC1-8) Although there are claims for prior dates, the earliest definite reference to cricket being played comes from evidence given at a court case in [Guildford](/wiki/Guildford "Guildford") in January 1597 ([Old Style](/wiki/Old_Style_and_New_Style_dates "Old Style and New Style dates"), equating to January 1598 in the modern calendar). The case concerned ownership of a certain plot of land, and the court heard the testimony of a 59-year-old [coroner](/wiki/Coroner "Coroner"), [John Derrick](/wiki/John_Derrick_(coroner) "John Derrick (coroner)"), who gave witness that:[[10]](#cite_note-HSA21-10)[[11]](#cite_note-DU3-11)[[12]](#cite_note-JM19-12)

> Being a scholler in the [ffree schoole of Guldeford](/wiki/Royal_Grammar_School,_Guildford "Royal Grammar School, Guildford") hee and diverse of his fellows did runne and play there at creckett and other plaies.

Given Derrick's age, it was about half a century earlier when he was at school, and so it is certain that cricket was being played c. 1550 by boys in [Surrey](/wiki/Surrey "Surrey").[[12]](#cite_note-JM19-12) The view that it was originally a children's game is reinforced by [Randle Cotgrave](/wiki/Randle_Cotgrave "Randle Cotgrave")'s 1611 English-[French](/wiki/French_language "French language") dictionary in which he defined the noun "*crosse*" as "the crooked staff wherewith boys play at cricket", and the verb form "*crosser*" as "to play at cricket".[[13]](#cite_note-HSA22-13)[[14]](#cite_note-JM31-14)

One possible source for the sport's name is the [Old English](/wiki/Old_English_language "Old English language") word "*cryce*" (or "*cricc*") meaning a crutch or staff. In [Samuel Johnson](/wiki/Samuel_Johnson "Samuel Johnson")'s *Dictionary*, he derived cricket from "*cryce*, Saxon, a stick".[[10]](#cite_note-HSA21-10) In [Old French](/wiki/Old_French "Old French"), the word "*criquet*" seems to have meant a kind of club or stick.[[15]](#cite_note-DB3-15) Given the strong medieval trade connections between south-east England and the [County of Flanders](/wiki/County_of_Flanders "County of Flanders") when the latter belonged to the [Duchy of Burgundy](/wiki/Duchy_of_Burgundy "Duchy of Burgundy"), the name may have been derived from the [Middle Dutch](/wiki/Middle_Dutch "Middle Dutch") (in use in Flanders at the time) "*krick*"(*-e*), meaning a stick (crook).[[15]](#cite_note-DB3-15) Another possible source is the Middle Dutch word "*krickstoel*", meaning a long low stool used for kneeling in church that resembled the long low [wicket](/wiki/Wicket "Wicket") with two [stumps](/wiki/Stump_(cricket) "Stump (cricket)") used in early cricket.[[16]](#cite_note-16) According to Heiner Gillmeister, a European language expert of [Bonn University](/wiki/Bonn_University "Bonn University"), "cricket" derives from the Middle Dutch phrase for hockey, "*met de (krik ket)sen*" ("with the stick chase").[[17]](#cite_note-17CGC-17) Gillmeister has suggested that not only the name but also the sport itself may be of Flemish origin.[[17]](#cite_note-17CGC-17)

### Growth of amateur and professional cricket in England

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/dc/Historical_cricket_bat_art.jpg/250px-Historical_cricket_bat_art.jpg)](/wiki/File:Historical_cricket_bat_art.jpg)

Evolution of the cricket bat. The original "[hockey stick](/wiki/Hockey_stick "Hockey stick")" (left) evolved into the straight bat from c. 1760, when [pitched delivery bowling](/wiki/Pitched_delivery_bowling "Pitched delivery bowling") began.

Although the main object of the game has always been to score the most [runs](/wiki/Run_(cricket) "Run (cricket)"), the early form of cricket differed from the modern game in certain key technical aspects; the North American variant of cricket known as [wicket](/wiki/Wicket_(sport) "Wicket (sport)") retained many of these aspects.[[18]](#cite_note-18) The [ball](/wiki/Cricket_ball "Cricket ball") was [bowled underarm](/wiki/Underarm_bowling "Underarm bowling") by the [bowler](/wiki/Bowling_(cricket) "Bowling (cricket)") and along the ground towards a [batter](/wiki/Batting_(cricket) "Batting (cricket)") armed with a [bat](/wiki/Cricket_bat "Cricket bat") that in shape resembled a [hockey stick](/wiki/Hockey_stick "Hockey stick"); the batter defended a low, two-stump [wicket](/wiki/Wicket "Wicket"); and runs were called notches because the [scorers](/wiki/Scoring_(cricket) "Scoring (cricket)") recorded them by notching tally sticks.[[19]](#cite_note-DB9-19)[[20]](#cite_note-BWC2-20)[[21]](#cite_note-JM21-21)

In 1611, the year [Cotgrave](/wiki/Randle_Cotgrave "Randle Cotgrave")'s dictionary was published, [ecclesiastical court](/wiki/Ecclesiastical_court "Ecclesiastical court") records at [Sidlesham](/wiki/Sidlesham "Sidlesham") in [Sussex](/wiki/Sussex "Sussex") state that two parishioners, Bartholomew Wyatt and Richard Latter, failed to attend church on [Easter Sunday](/wiki/Easter_Sunday "Easter Sunday") because they were playing cricket. They were fined 12[d](/wiki/Penny_(British_pre-decimal_coin) "Penny (British pre-decimal coin)") each and ordered to do [penance](/wiki/Penance "Penance").[[22]](#cite_note-22) This is the earliest mention of adult participation in cricket and it was around the same time that the earliest known organised inter-parish or [village](/wiki/Village_cricket "Village cricket") match was played, at [Chevening, Kent](/wiki/Chevening,_Kent "Chevening, Kent").[[10]](#cite_note-HSA21-10)[[23]](#cite_note-DU4-23) In 1624, a player called [Jasper Vinall](/wiki/Jasper_Vinall "Jasper Vinall") died after he was accidentally struck on the head during a match between two parish teams in Sussex.[[24]](#cite_note-24)

Cricket remained a low-key local pursuit for much of the 17th century.[[14]](#cite_note-JM31-14) It is known, through numerous references found in the records of ecclesiastical court cases, to have been proscribed at times by the [Puritans](/wiki/Puritans "Puritans") before and during the [Commonwealth](/wiki/Commonwealth_of_England "Commonwealth of England").[[25]](#cite_note-25)[[26]](#cite_note-DU11-26) The problem was nearly always the issue of Sunday play, as the Puritans considered cricket to be "profane" if played on the [Sabbath](/wiki/Sabbath "Sabbath"), especially if large crowds or [gambling](/wiki/Sport_gambling "Sport gambling") were involved.[[27]](#cite_note-DB7-27)[[28]](#cite_note-JM23-28)

According to the social historian [Derek Birley](/wiki/Derek_Birley "Derek Birley"), there was a "great upsurge of sport after the [Restoration](/wiki/English_Restoration "English Restoration")" in 1660.[[29]](#cite_note-DB11-29) Several members of the court of King [Charles II](/wiki/Charles_II_of_England,_Scotland_and_Ireland "Charles II of England, Scotland and Ireland") took a strong interest in cricket during that era.[[30]](#cite_note-30) Gambling on sport became a problem significant enough for [Parliament](/wiki/Parliament_of_the_United_Kingdom "Parliament of the United Kingdom") to pass the 1664 Gambling Act, limiting stakes to [£](/wiki/Pound_sterling "Pound sterling")100, which was, in any case, a colossal sum exceeding the annual income of 99% of the population.[[29]](#cite_note-DB11-29) Along with [horse racing](/wiki/Horse_racing "Horse racing"), as well as [prizefighting](/wiki/Prizefighting "Prizefighting") and other types of [blood sport](/wiki/Blood_sport "Blood sport"), cricket was perceived to be a gambling sport.[[31]](#cite_note-31) Rich patrons made matches for high stakes, forming teams in which they engaged the first professional players.[[32]](#cite_note-RW10-32) By the end of the century, cricket had developed into a major sport that was spreading throughout England and was already being taken abroad by English mariners and colonisers—the earliest reference to cricket overseas is dated 1676.[[33]](#cite_note-33) A 1697 newspaper report survives of "a great cricket match" played in Sussex "for fifty guineas apiece", the earliest known contest that is generally considered a [First Class](/wiki/First-class_cricket "First-class cricket") match.[[34]](#cite_note-34)[[35]](#cite_note-35)

The patrons and other players from the [gentry](/wiki/Gentry "Gentry") began to classify themselves as "[amateurs](/wiki/Amateur_status_in_first-class_cricket "Amateur status in first-class cricket")"[[fn 1]](#cite_note-amateur-36) to establish a clear distinction from the professionals, who were invariably members of the [working class](/wiki/Working_class "Working class"), even to the point of having separate changing and dining facilities.[[36]](#cite_note-37) The gentry, including such high-ranking nobles as the [Dukes of Richmond](/wiki/Duke_of_Richmond "Duke of Richmond"), exerted their honour code of *[noblesse oblige](/wiki/Noblesse_oblige "Noblesse oblige")* to claim rights of leadership in any sporting contests they took part in, especially as it was necessary for them to play alongside their "social inferiors" if they were to win their bets.[[37]](#cite_note-DB19-38) In time, a perception took hold that the typical amateur who played in first-class cricket, until 1962 when amateurism was abolished, was someone with a [public school](/wiki/Public_school_(United_Kingdom) "Public school (United Kingdom)") education who had then gone to one of [Cambridge](/wiki/University_of_Cambridge "University of Cambridge") or [Oxford University](/wiki/University_of_Oxford "University of Oxford"). Society insisted that such people were "officers and gentlemen" whose destiny was to provide leadership.[[38]](#cite_note-CW23-39) In a purely financial sense, the cricketing amateur would theoretically claim expenses for playing while his professional counterpart played under contract and was paid a wage or match fee; in practice, many amateurs claimed more than actual expenditure, and the derisive term "shamateur" was coined to describe the practice.[[39]](#cite_note-40)[[40]](#cite_note-DB146-41)

### English cricket in the 18th and 19th centuries

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a9/Francis_Cotes_-_The_young_cricketer_%281768%29.jpg/250px-Francis_Cotes_-_The_young_cricketer_%281768%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Francis_Cotes_-_The_young_cricketer_(1768).jpg)

*[The Young Cricketer](/wiki/The_Young_Cricketer "The Young Cricketer")* ([Francis Cotes](/wiki/Francis_Cotes "Francis Cotes"), 1768)

The game underwent major development in the 18th century to become England's [national sport](/wiki/National_sport "National sport").[[41]](#cite_note-42) Its success was underwritten by the twin necessities of patronage and betting.[[42]](#cite_note-43) Cricket was prominent in [London](/wiki/London "London") as early as 1707 and, in the middle years of the century, large crowds flocked to matches on the [Artillery Ground](/wiki/Artillery_Ground "Artillery Ground") in [Finsbury](/wiki/Finsbury "Finsbury").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] The [single wicket](/wiki/Single_wicket_cricket "Single wicket cricket") form of the sport attracted huge crowds and wagers to match, its popularity peaking in the [1748 season](/wiki/1748_English_cricket_season "1748 English cricket season").[[43]](#cite_note-ASW-44) Bowling underwent an evolution around 1760 when bowlers began to [pitch (bounce) the ball](/wiki/Pitched_delivery_bowling "Pitched delivery bowling") instead of rolling or skimming it towards the batter. This caused a revolution in bat design because, to deal with the bouncing ball, it was necessary to introduce the modern straight bat in place of the old "hockey stick" shape.[[44]](#cite_note-45)[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The [Hambledon Club](/wiki/Hambledon_Club "Hambledon Club") was founded in the 1760s and, for the next twenty years until the formation of [Marylebone Cricket Club](/wiki/Marylebone_Cricket_Club "Marylebone Cricket Club") (MCC) and the opening of [Lord's Old Ground](/wiki/Lord%27s_Old_Ground "Lord's Old Ground") in 1787, Hambledon was both the game's greatest club and its focal point.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] MCC quickly became the sport's premier club and the custodian of the *[Laws of Cricket](/wiki/Laws_of_Cricket "Laws of Cricket")*. New Laws introduced in the latter part of the 18th century include the three-stump wicket and [leg before wicket](/wiki/Leg_before_wicket "Leg before wicket") (lbw).[[45]](#cite_note-WCA63-46)

The 19th century saw [underarm bowling](/wiki/Underarm_bowling "Underarm bowling") superseded by first [roundarm](/wiki/Roundarm_bowling "Roundarm bowling") and then [overarm bowling](/wiki/Overarm_bowling "Overarm bowling"). Both developments were controversial.[[46]](#cite_note-47) Organisation of the game at county level led to the creation of the [county clubs](/wiki/County_cricket_club "County cricket club"), starting with [Sussex](/wiki/Sussex_County_Cricket_Club "Sussex County Cricket Club") in 1839.[[47]](#cite_note-48) In December 1889, the eight leading county clubs formed the official [County Championship](/wiki/County_Championship "County Championship"), which began in 1890.[[48]](#cite_note-AMCS-49)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d1/First_photo_of_a_cricket_match_by_Roger_Fenton.jpg/250px-First_photo_of_a_cricket_match_by_Roger_Fenton.jpg)](/wiki/File:First_photo_of_a_cricket_match_by_Roger_Fenton.jpg)

The first recorded photo of a cricket match taken on 25 July 1857 by [Roger Fenton](/wiki/Roger_Fenton "Roger Fenton")

The most famous player of the 19th century was [W. G. Grace](/wiki/W._G._Grace "W. G. Grace"), who started his long and influential career in 1865. It was especially during the career of Grace that the distinction between amateurs and professionals became blurred by the existence of players like him who were nominally amateur but, in terms of their financial gain, *de facto* professional. Grace himself was said to have been paid more money for playing cricket than any professional.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The last two decades before the [First World War](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I") have been called the "[Golden Age of cricket](/wiki/Golden_Age_of_cricket "Golden Age of cricket")". It is a nostalgic name prompted by the collective sense of loss resulting from the war, but the period did produce some great players and memorable matches, especially as organised competition at county and [Test level](/wiki/Test_cricket "Test cricket") developed.[[49]](#cite_note-50)

### Cricket becomes an international sport

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/84/England_in_North_America_1859.jpg/250px-England_in_North_America_1859.jpg)](/wiki/File:England_in_North_America_1859.jpg)

The first English team to tour overseas, on board ship to North America, 1859

Meanwhile, the [British Empire](/wiki/British_Empire "British Empire") had been instrumental in spreading the game overseas, and by the middle of the 19th century it had become well established in [Australia](/wiki/Australia "Australia"), the [Caribbean](/wiki/Caribbean "Caribbean"), [British India](/wiki/British_India "British India") (which also includes present-day [Pakistan](/wiki/Pakistan "Pakistan") and [Bangladesh](/wiki/Bangladesh "Bangladesh")), [New Zealand](/wiki/New_Zealand "New Zealand"), [North America](/wiki/North_America "North America") and [South Africa](/wiki/South_Africa "South Africa").[[50]](#cite_note-51) In 1844, the [first international match](/wiki/United_States_v_Canada_(1844) "United States v Canada (1844)") took place between what were essentially club teams,[[51]](#cite_note-NYH-25-09-1844-52) from the [United States](/wiki/United_States_national_cricket_team "United States national cricket team") and [Canada](/wiki/Canada_national_cricket_team "Canada national cricket team"), in [New York](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City"); Canada won.[[52]](#cite_note-53)[[53]](#cite_note-54) In 1859, a team of English players went to North America on [the first overseas tour](/wiki/English_cricket_team_in_North_America_in_1859 "English cricket team in North America in 1859").[[54]](#cite_note-55)

In 1862, an English team made the first tour of Australia.[[55]](#cite_note-56) The first Australian team to travel overseas consisted of [Aboriginal](/wiki/Indigenous_Australians "Indigenous Australians") [stockmen](/wiki/Stockman_(Australia) "Stockman (Australia)") who [toured England in 1868](/wiki/Australian_Aboriginal_cricket_team_in_England_in_1868 "Australian Aboriginal cricket team in England in 1868").[[56]](#cite_note-57)

In 1876–77, an [England team](/wiki/England_cricket_team "England cricket team") took part in what was retrospectively recognised as the first-ever [Test match](/wiki/Test_cricket "Test cricket") at the [Melbourne Cricket Ground](/wiki/Melbourne_Cricket_Ground "Melbourne Cricket Ground") against [Australia](/wiki/Australia_national_cricket_team "Australia national cricket team").[[57]](#cite_note-58) The rivalry between England and Australia gave birth to [The Ashes](/wiki/The_Ashes "The Ashes") in 1882, which remains Test cricket's most famous contest.[[58]](#cite_note-59) Test cricket began to expand in 1888–89 when [South Africa](/wiki/South_Africa_national_cricket_team "South Africa national cricket team") played England.[[59]](#cite_note-60)

### Cricket in the 20th century

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/19/Bradman%26Bat.jpg/250px-Bradman%26Bat.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bradman%26Bat.jpg)

[Don Bradman](/wiki/Don_Bradman "Don Bradman") of [Australia](/wiki/Australia_national_cricket_team "Australia national cricket team") had a record Test [batting average](/wiki/Batting_average "Batting average") of 99.94.

The inter-war years were dominated by [Australia](/wiki/Australia_national_cricket_team "Australia national cricket team")'s [Don Bradman](/wiki/Don_Bradman "Don Bradman"), statistically the greatest Test batter of all time. To curb his dominance, England employed [bodyline](/wiki/Bodyline "Bodyline") tactics during the [1932–33 Ashes series](/wiki/English_cricket_team_in_Australia_in_1932%E2%80%9333 "English cricket team in Australia in 1932–33"). These involved bowling at the body of the batter and setting a field, resulting in batters having to choose between being hit or risk getting out. This series moved cricket from a game to a matter of [national importance](/wiki/National_sport "National sport"), with diplomatic cables being passed between the two countries over the incident.[[60]](#cite_note-61)

During this time, the number of Test nations continued to grow, with the [West Indies](/wiki/West_Indies_Cricket_Team "West Indies Cricket Team"), [New Zealand](/wiki/New_Zealand_Cricket_Team "New Zealand Cricket Team") and [India](/wiki/Indian_Cricket_Team "Indian Cricket Team") being admitted as full Test members within a four-year period from 1928 to 1932.

An enforced break during the [Second World War](/wiki/Second_World_War "Second World War") stopped Test Cricket for a time, although the [Partition of India](/wiki/Partition_of_India "Partition of India") caused [Pakistan](/wiki/Pakistan_Cricket_Team "Pakistan Cricket Team") to gain Test status in 1952. As teams began to travel more, the game quickly grew from 500 tests in 84 years to 1000 within the next 23.

Cricket entered a new era in 1963 when English counties introduced the [limited overs](/wiki/Limited_overs_cricket "Limited overs cricket") variant.[[61]](#cite_note-62) As it was sure to produce a result, limited overs cricket was lucrative, and the number of matches increased.[[62]](#cite_note-63) The first [Limited Overs International](/wiki/Limited_Overs_International "Limited Overs International") was played in 1971, and the governing [International Cricket Council](/wiki/International_Cricket_Council "International Cricket Council") (ICC), seeing its potential, staged the first limited overs [Cricket World Cup](/wiki/Cricket_World_Cup "Cricket World Cup") in [1975](/wiki/1975_Cricket_World_Cup "1975 Cricket World Cup").[[63]](#cite_note-64)

[Sri Lanka](/wiki/Sri_Lankan_Cricket_Team "Sri Lankan Cricket Team") joined the ranks in 1982. Meanwhile, South Africa was banned by the ICC due to [apartheid](/wiki/Apartheid "Apartheid") from 1970 until 1992. 1992 also brought about the introduction of the [Zimbabwe team](/wiki/Zimbabwe_Cricket_Team "Zimbabwe Cricket Team").[[64]](#cite_note-65)

### Cricket in the 21st century

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/91/SRH_fans_while_an_ipl_match.jpg/250px-SRH_fans_while_an_ipl_match.jpg)](/wiki/File:SRH_fans_while_an_ipl_match.jpg)

The [Indian Premier League](/wiki/Indian_Premier_League "Indian Premier League") (IPL) was launched in 2008. It has become one of the richest sports leagues in the world, and has greatly increased the importance of T20 cricket and franchise leagues.[[65]](#cite_note-66)

The 21st century brought with it the [Bangladesh Team](/wiki/Bangladesh_Cricket_Team "Bangladesh Cricket Team"), who made their Test debut in 2000. The game itself also grew, with a new format made up of 20-over innings being created. This format, called [T20 cricket](/wiki/T20_cricket "T20 cricket"), quickly became a highly popular format, putting the longer formats at risk. The new shorter format also introduced [franchise](/wiki/Franchise_(sport) "Franchise (sport)") cricket, with new tournaments like the Indian Premier League and the Australian [Big Bash League](/wiki/Big_Bash_League "Big Bash League"). The ICC has selected the T20 format as cricket's growth format, and has introduced a [T20 World Cup](/wiki/Men%27s_T20_World_Cup "Men's T20 World Cup") which is played every two years;[[66]](#cite_note-67) T20 cricket has also been increasingly accepted into major events such as the [Asian Games](/wiki/Asian_Games "Asian Games").[[67]](#cite_note-68) The resultant growth has seen cricket's fanbase cross one billion people, with [90% of them](/wiki/Cricket_in_South_Asia "Cricket in South Asia") in South Asia.[[2]](#cite_note-:0-2) T20's success has also spawned even [shorter formats](/wiki/Short_form_cricket "Short form cricket"), such as [10-over cricket](/wiki/T10_cricket "T10 cricket") (T10) and [100-ball cricket](/wiki/100-ball_cricket "100-ball cricket"), though not without controversy.[[68]](#cite_note-69)

Outside factors have also taken their toll on cricket. For example, the [2008 Mumbai attacks](/wiki/2008_Mumbai_attacks "2008 Mumbai attacks") led India and Pakistan to suspend their [bilateral series](/wiki/India%E2%80%93Pakistan_cricket_rivalry "India–Pakistan cricket rivalry") indefinitely. The [2009 attack on the Sri Lankan team](/wiki/2009_attack_on_the_Sri_Lanka_national_cricket_team "2009 attack on the Sri Lanka national cricket team") during their tour of Pakistan led to Pakistan being unable to host matches until 2019.[[69]](#cite_note-70)[[70]](#cite_note-71)[[71]](#cite_note-72)[[72]](#cite_note-73)

In 2017, [Afghanistan](/wiki/Afghanistan_national_cricket_team "Afghanistan national cricket team") and [Ireland](/wiki/Ireland_cricket_team "Ireland cricket team") became the 11th and 12th Test nations.[[73]](#cite_note-74)[[74]](#cite_note-75)

## Laws and gameplay

Main article: [Laws of Cricket](/wiki/Laws_of_Cricket "Laws of Cricket")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2c/Cricket_field_parts.svg/250px-Cricket_field_parts.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Cricket_field_parts.svg)

A typical [cricket field](/wiki/Cricket_field "Cricket field")

In cricket, the rules of the game are codified in *[The Laws of Cricket](/wiki/The_Laws_of_Cricket "The Laws of Cricket")* (hereinafter called "the Laws"), which has a global remit. There are 42 Laws (always written with a capital "L"). The earliest known version of the code was drafted in 1744, and since 1788, it has been owned and maintained by its custodian, the [Marylebone Cricket Club](/wiki/Marylebone_Cricket_Club "Marylebone Cricket Club") (MCC) in [London](/wiki/London "London").[[75]](#cite_note-Laws-76)

### Playing area

Main articles: [Cricket field](/wiki/Cricket_field "Cricket field"), [Cricket pitch](/wiki/Cricket_pitch "Cricket pitch"), [Crease (cricket)](/wiki/Crease_(cricket) "Crease (cricket)"), and [Wicket](/wiki/Wicket "Wicket")

Cricket is a [bat-and-ball game](/wiki/Bat-and-ball_games "Bat-and-ball games") played on a [cricket field](/wiki/Cricket_field "Cricket field") (see image of cricket pitch and creases) between two teams of eleven players each.[[76]](#cite_note-Law1-77) The field is usually circular or oval in shape, and the edge of the playing area is marked by a [boundary](/wiki/Boundary_(cricket) "Boundary (cricket)"), which may be a fence, part of the stands, a rope, a painted line, or a combination of these; the boundary must if possible be marked along its entire length.[[77]](#cite_note-78)

In the approximate centre of the field is a rectangular [pitch](/wiki/Cricket_pitch "Cricket pitch") (see image, below) on which a wooden target called a [wicket](/wiki/Wicket "Wicket") is sited at each end; the wickets are placed 22 yards (20 m) apart.[[78]](#cite_note-79) The pitch is a flat surface 10 feet (3.0 m) wide, with very short grass that tends to be worn away as the game progresses (cricket can also be played on artificial surfaces, notably matting). Each wicket is made of three wooden [stumps](/wiki/Stump_(cricket) "Stump (cricket)") topped by two [bails](/wiki/Bail_(cricket) "Bail (cricket)").[[79]](#cite_note-80)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cb/Cricket_pitch.svg/960px-Cricket_pitch.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Cricket_pitch.svg)

Cricket pitch and creases

As illustrated, the pitch is marked at each end with four white painted lines: a [bowling crease](/wiki/Crease_(cricket)#Bowling_crease "Crease (cricket)"), a [popping crease](/wiki/Crease_(cricket)#Popping_crease "Crease (cricket)") and two [return creases](/wiki/Crease_(cricket)#Return_crease "Crease (cricket)"). The three stumps are aligned centrally on the bowling crease, which is eight feet eight inches long. The popping crease is drawn four feet in front of the bowling crease and parallel to it; although it is drawn as a 12 ft (3.7 m) line (six feet on either side of the wicket), it is, in fact, unlimited in length. The return creases are drawn at right angles to the popping crease so that they intersect the ends of the bowling crease; each return crease is drawn as an 8 ft (2.4 m) line, so that it extends four feet behind the bowling crease, but is also, in fact, unlimited in length.[[80]](#cite_note-81)

### Match structure

Main article: [Innings](/wiki/Innings "Innings")

Before a match begins, the team [captains](/wiki/Captain_(cricket) "Captain (cricket)") (who are also players) [toss a coin](/wiki/Toss_(cricket) "Toss (cricket)") to decide which team will [bat](/wiki/Batting_(cricket) "Batting (cricket)") first and so take the first [innings](/wiki/Innings "Innings").[[81]](#cite_note-inns-82) "Innings" is the term used for each phase of play in the match.[[81]](#cite_note-inns-82) In each innings, one team bats, attempting to [score](/wiki/Scoring_(cricket) "Scoring (cricket)") [runs](/wiki/Run_(cricket) "Run (cricket)"), while the other team [bowls](/wiki/Bowling_(cricket) "Bowling (cricket)") and [fields](/wiki/Fielding_(cricket) "Fielding (cricket)") the [ball](/wiki/Cricket_ball "Cricket ball"), attempting to restrict the scoring and [dismiss](/wiki/Dismissal_(cricket) "Dismissal (cricket)") the batters.[[82]](#cite_note-83)[[83]](#cite_note-84) When the first innings ends, the teams change roles; there can be two to four innings depending upon the type of match. A match with four scheduled innings is played over three to five days; a match with two scheduled innings is usually completed in a single day.[[81]](#cite_note-inns-82) During an innings, all eleven members of the fielding team take the field, but usually only two members of the batting team are on the field at any given time.[[a]](#cite_note-87) The order of batters is usually announced just before the match, but it can be varied.[[76]](#cite_note-Law1-77)

The main objective of each team is to score more runs than their opponents, but in some forms of cricket, it is also necessary to dismiss all but one of the opposition batters (making their team 'all out') in their final innings in order to win the match, which would otherwise be [drawn](/wiki/Result_(cricket)#Draw "Result (cricket)") (not ending with a winner or tie.)[[86]](#cite_note-result-88)

### Clothing and equipment

Main article: [Cricket clothing and equipment](/wiki/Cricket_clothing_and_equipment "Cricket clothing and equipment")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2e/WGGrace.jpg/250px-WGGrace.jpg)](/wiki/File:WGGrace.jpg)

English cricketer [W. G. Grace](/wiki/W._G._Grace "W. G. Grace") "taking guard" in 1883. His pads and bat are very similar to those used today. The gloves have evolved somewhat. Many modern players use more defensive equipment than were available to Grace, most notably helmets and arm guards.

The [wicket-keeper](/wiki/Wicket-keeper "Wicket-keeper") (a specialised fielder behind the batter) and the batters wear protective gear because of the hardness of the ball, which can be delivered at speeds of more than 145 kilometres per hour (90 mph) and presents a major health and safety concern. Protective clothing includes [pads](/wiki/Batting_pads "Batting pads") (designed to protect the knees and shins), [batting gloves](/wiki/Batting_gloves "Batting gloves") or [wicket-keeper's gloves](/wiki/Wicket-keeper%27s_gloves "Wicket-keeper's gloves") for the hands, a [safety helmet](/wiki/Helmet_(cricket) "Helmet (cricket)") for the head, and a [box](/wiki/Box_(cricket) "Box (cricket)") for male players inside the trousers (to protect the [crotch](/wiki/Crotch "Crotch") area).[[87]](#cite_note-AppD-89) Some batters wear additional padding inside their shirts and trousers such as thigh pads, arm pads, rib protectors and shoulder pads. The only fielders allowed to wear protective gear are those in positions very close to the batter (i.e., if they are alongside or in front of him), but they cannot wear gloves or external leg guards.[[88]](#cite_note-Law41-90)

Subject to certain variations, on-field clothing generally includes a collared shirt with short or long sleeves; long trousers; woolen pullover (if needed); [cricket cap](/wiki/Cricket_cap "Cricket cap") (for fielding) or a safety helmet; and spiked shoes or boots to increase traction. The [kit](/wiki/Cricket_kits "Cricket kits") is traditionally all white, and this remains the case in Test and first-class cricket, but in limited overs cricket, team colours are now worn instead.[[89]](#cite_note-91)

#### Bat and ball

Main articles: [Cricket bat](/wiki/Cricket_bat "Cricket bat") and [Cricket ball](/wiki/Cricket_ball "Cricket ball")

[![New white ball](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3b/Cricket_ball_white_%282025%29_01_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Cricket_ball_white_%282025%29_01_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Cricket_ball_white_(2025)_01_(cropped).jpg)

[![New red ball](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8f/Cricket_Ball_%2827343391052%29_cropped.jpg/250px-Cricket_Ball_%2827343391052%29_cropped.jpg)](/wiki/File:Cricket_Ball_(27343391052)_cropped.jpg)

[![Used pink ball](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8c/Cricket_ball_at_Church_Times_Cricket_Cup_final_2019_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Cricket_ball_at_Church_Times_Cricket_Cup_final_2019_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Cricket_ball_at_Church_Times_Cricket_Cup_final_2019_(cropped).jpg)

The three types of [cricket balls](/wiki/Cricket_ball "Cricket ball") used in international matches, all of the same size:

i) A new white ball. White balls are mainly used in [limited overs cricket](/wiki/Limited_overs_cricket "Limited overs cricket"), especially in matches played at night, under [floodlights](/wiki/Floodlights "Floodlights") (left).  
ii) A new red ball. Red balls are used in day [Test cricket](/wiki/Test_cricket "Test cricket"), [first-class cricket](/wiki/First-class_cricket "First-class cricket") and some other forms of cricket (center).

iii) A used pink ball. Pink balls are used in [day/night Test cricket](/wiki/Day/night_cricket "Day/night cricket") (right).

The essence of the sport is that a [bowler](/wiki/Bowling_(cricket) "Bowling (cricket)") [delivers](/wiki/Delivery_(cricket) "Delivery (cricket)") (i.e., bowls) the [ball](/wiki/Cricket_ball "Cricket ball") from their end of the [pitch](/wiki/Cricket_pitch "Cricket pitch") towards the [batter](/wiki/Batting_(cricket) "Batting (cricket)") who, armed with a [bat](/wiki/Cricket_bat "Cricket bat"), is "on strike" at the other end (see next sub-section: *[Basic gameplay](#Basic_gameplay:_bowler_to_batter)*).

The [bat](/wiki/Cricket_bat "Cricket bat") is made of wood, usually *[Salix alba](/wiki/Salix_alba "Salix alba")* (white willow), and has the shape of a blade topped by a cylindrical handle. The blade must not be more than 4.25 inches (10.8 cm) wide and the total length of the bat not more than 38 inches (97 cm). There is no standard for the weight, which is usually between 2 lb 7 oz and 3 lb (1.1 and 1.4 kg).[[90]](#cite_note-Law6-92)[[91]](#cite_note-93)

The [ball](/wiki/Cricket_ball "Cricket ball") is a hard leather-seamed [spheroid](/wiki/Spheroid "Spheroid"), with a circumference of 9 inches (23 cm). The ball has a "seam": six rows of stitches attaching the leather shell of the ball to the string and cork interior. The seam on a new ball is prominent and helps the bowler propel it in a less predictable manner. During matches, the quality of the ball deteriorates to a point where it is no longer usable; during the course of this deterioration, its behaviour in flight will change and can influence the outcome of the match. Players will, therefore, attempt to modify the ball's behaviour by modifying its physical properties. Polishing the ball and wetting it with sweat or saliva was legal, even when the polishing was deliberately done on one side only to increase the ball's [swing through the air](/wiki/Swing_bowling "Swing bowling"). The use of saliva has since been made illegal due to the [COVID-19 pandemic](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic "COVID-19 pandemic").[[92]](#cite_note-94) The acts of rubbing other substances into the ball, scratching the surface or picking at the seams constitute illegal [ball tampering](/wiki/Ball_tampering "Ball tampering").[[93]](#cite_note-Law5-95)

### Player roles

#### Basic gameplay: bowler to batter

During normal play, thirteen players and two [umpires](/wiki/Umpire_(cricket) "Umpire (cricket)") are on the field. Two of the players are [batters](/wiki/Batting_(cricket) "Batting (cricket)") and the rest are all eleven members of the [fielding](/wiki/Fielding_(cricket) "Fielding (cricket)") team. The other nine players in the batting team are off the field in the [pavilion](/wiki/Cricket_pavilion "Cricket pavilion"). The image with overlay below shows what is happening when a ball is being bowled and which of the personnel are on or close to the [pitch](/wiki/Cricket_pitch "Cricket pitch").[[94]](#cite_note-96)

In the photo, the two batters (3 and 8, wearing yellow) have taken position at each end of the pitch (6). Three members of the fielding team (4, 10 and 11, wearing dark blue) are in shot. One of the two umpires (1, wearing white hat) is stationed behind the [wicket](/wiki/Wicket "Wicket") (2) at the [bowler](/wiki/Bowling_(cricket) "Bowling (cricket)")'s (4) end of the pitch. The bowler (4) is [bowling](/wiki/Delivery_(cricket) "Delivery (cricket)") the [ball](/wiki/Cricket_ball "Cricket ball") (5) from his end of the pitch to the batter (8) at the other end who is called the "striker". The other batter (3) at the bowling end is called the "non-striker". The [wicket-keeper](/wiki/Wicket-keeper "Wicket-keeper") (10), who is a specialist, is positioned behind the striker's wicket (9), and behind him stands one of the fielders in a position called "[first slip](/wiki/Fielding_(cricket)#Catching_positions "Fielding (cricket)")" (11). While the bowler and the first slip are wearing conventional kit only, the two batters and the wicket-keeper are wearing protective gear, including safety helmets, padded gloves and leg guards (pads). The wicket-keeper is the only fielding player able to wear protective gloves.

While the umpire (1) in shot stands at the bowler's end of the pitch, his colleague stands in the outfield, usually in or near the fielding position called "[square leg](/wiki/Square_leg "Square leg")", so that he is in line with the [popping crease](/wiki/Popping_crease "Popping crease") (7) at the striker's end of the pitch. The bowling crease (not numbered) is the one on which the wicket is located between the return creases (12). The bowler (4) intends to hit the wicket (9) with the ball (5) or at least prevent the striker (8) from scoring [runs](/wiki/Run_(cricket) "Run (cricket)"). The striker (8) intends, by using his bat, to defend his wicket and, if possible, hit the ball away from the pitch in order to score runs.

Some players are skilled in both batting and bowling, so are termed [all-rounders](/wiki/All-rounder "All-rounder"). Bowlers are classified according to their style and speed, generally as [fast bowlers](/wiki/Fast_bowling "Fast bowling"), [seam bowlers](/wiki/Seam_bowling "Seam bowling") or [spinners](/wiki/Spin_bowling "Spin bowling"). Batters are classified according to whether they are right-handed or left-handed, with [switch-hitting](/wiki/Switch_hit "Switch hit") uncommon and largely utilised as a tactic, where a batter changes stance shortly before the bowler releases the ball.[[95]](#cite_note-97)

##### Overs

Main article: [Over (cricket)](/wiki/Over_(cricket) "Over (cricket)")

The Laws state that, throughout an innings, "the ball shall be bowled from each end alternately in overs of 6 balls".[[96]](#cite_note-Law22-98) The name "over" came about because the umpire calls "Over!" when six legal balls (deliveries) have been bowled. At this point, another bowler is deployed at the other end, and the fielding side changes ends while the batters do not. A bowler cannot bowl two successive overs, although a bowler can (and usually does) bowl alternate overs, from the same end, for several overs which are termed a "spell"; if the captain wants a bowler to "change ends", another bowler must temporarily fill in so that the change is not immediate. The batters do not change ends at the end of the over, and so the one who was non-striker is now the striker and vice versa. The umpires also change positions so that the one who was at "[square leg](/wiki/Square_leg "Square leg")" now stands behind the wicket at the nonstriker's end and vice versa.[[96]](#cite_note-Law22-98)

#### Fielding

Main article: [Fielding (cricket)](/wiki/Fielding_(cricket) "Fielding (cricket)")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9a/Cricket_fielding_positions2.svg/330px-Cricket_fielding_positions2.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Cricket_fielding_positions2.svg)

[Fielding positions in cricket](/wiki/Fielding_positions_in_cricket "Fielding positions in cricket") for a right-handed batter

Of the eleven fielders, three are in shot in the image above. The other eight are elsewhere on the field, their positions determined on a tactical basis by the captain or the bowler. Fielders often change position between deliveries, again as directed by the captain or bowler.[[88]](#cite_note-Law41-90)

If a fielder is injured or becomes ill during a match, a [substitute](/wiki/Substitute_(cricket) "Substitute (cricket)") is allowed to field instead of the aforementioned fielder, but the substitute cannot bowl or act as a captain, except in the case of concussion substitutes in international cricket.[[85]](#cite_note-ICCTestPlayCond-86) The substitute leaves the field when the injured player is fit to return.[[97]](#cite_note-Law2-99) The *Laws of Cricket* were updated in 2017 to allow substitutes to act as wicket-keepers.[[98]](#cite_note-100)

#### Batting and scoring

Main articles: [Batting (cricket)](/wiki/Batting_(cricket) "Batting (cricket)"), [Run (cricket)](/wiki/Run_(cricket) "Run (cricket)"), and [Extra (cricket)](/wiki/Extra_(cricket) "Extra (cricket)")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/94/Cricket_shots.svg/250px-Cricket_shots.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Cricket_shots.svg)

The directions in which a right-handed batter, facing down the page, intends to send the ball when playing various cricketing shots. The diagram for a [left-handed](/wiki/Left-handed "Left-handed") batter is a [mirror image](/wiki/Mirror_image "Mirror image") of this one.

Batters take turns to bat via a [batting order](/wiki/Batting_order_(cricket) "Batting order (cricket)") which is decided beforehand by the team captain and presented to the umpires, though the order remains flexible when the captain officially nominates the team.[[76]](#cite_note-Law1-77) Substitute batters are generally not allowed,[[97]](#cite_note-Law2-99) except in the case of concussion substitutes in international cricket.[[85]](#cite_note-ICCTestPlayCond-86)

In order to begin batting the batter first adopts a batting stance. Standardly, this involves adopting a slight crouch with the feet pointing across the front of the wicket, looking in the direction of the bowler, and holding the bat so it passes over the feet and so its tip can rest on the ground near to the toes of the back foot.[[99]](#cite_note-101)

A skilled batter can use a wide array of "shots" or "strokes" in both defensive and attacking mode. The idea is to hit the ball to the best effect with the flat surface of the bat's blade. If the ball touches the side of the bat, it is called an "[edge](/wiki/Edge_(cricket) "Edge (cricket)")". The batter does not have to play a shot and can allow the ball to go through to the [wicket-keeper](/wiki/Wicket-keeper "Wicket-keeper"). Equally, the batter does not have to attempt a run when hitting the ball with their bat. Batters do not always seek to hit the ball as hard as possible, and a good player can score runs by simply making a deft stroke with a turn of the wrists, or by simply "blocking" the ball but directing it away from fielders so that the player has time to take a run. A wide variety of shots are played, the batter's repertoire including strokes named according to the style of swing and the direction aimed: e.g., "[cut](/wiki/Cut_(cricket) "Cut (cricket)")", "drive", "hook", and "pull".[[100]](#cite_note-102)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/72/Master_Blaster_at_work.jpg/250px-Master_Blaster_at_work.jpg)](/wiki/File:Master_Blaster_at_work.jpg)

[Sachin Tendulkar](/wiki/Sachin_Tendulkar "Sachin Tendulkar") is the only player to have scored one hundred international [centuries](/wiki/Century_(cricket) "Century (cricket)").

The batter on strike (i.e., the "striker") must prevent the ball from hitting the wicket and try to score [runs](/wiki/Run_(cricket) "Run (cricket)") by hitting the ball with their bat so that the batter and their partner have time to switch places, with each of them running from one end of the pitch to the other before the fielding side can return the ball and attempt a [run out](/wiki/Run_out "Run out") (throwing the ball at one of the wickets before the run is scored.) To register a run, both runners must touch the [ground](/wiki/Batter%27s_ground "Batter's ground") behind the popping crease with either their bats or their bodies (the batters carry their bats as they run) before a fielder can throw the ball at the nearby wicket. Each completed run increments the score of both the team and the striker.[[101]](#cite_note-Law18-103)

The decision to attempt a run is ideally made by the batter who has the better view of the ball's progress, and this is communicated by calling, usually "yes", "no" or "wait". More than one run can be scored from a single hit. Hits worth one to three runs are common, but the size of the field is such that it is usually difficult to run four or more.[[101]](#cite_note-Law18-103) To compensate for this, hits that [reach the boundary](/wiki/Boundary_(scoring) "Boundary (scoring)") of the field are automatically awarded four runs if the ball touches the ground *en route* to the boundary or six runs if the ball clears the boundary without touching the ground within the boundary. In these cases the batters do not need to run.[[102]](#cite_note-Law19-104) Hits for five are unusual and generally rely on the help of "overthrows" by a fielder returning the ball.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/90/Cambridge_University_CC_v_MCC_at_Cambridge%2C_England_018.jpg/250px-Cambridge_University_CC_v_MCC_at_Cambridge%2C_England_018.jpg)](/wiki/File:Cambridge_University_CC_v_MCC_at_Cambridge,_England_018.jpg)

Batters attempting a run while a fielder awaits a throw.

If an odd number of runs is scored by the striker, the two batters have changed ends, and the one who was non-striker is now the striker. Only the striker can score individual runs, but all runs are added to the team's total.[[101]](#cite_note-Law18-103)

Additional runs can be gained by the batting team as [extras](/wiki/Extra_(cricket) "Extra (cricket)") (called "sundries" in Australia) due to errors made by the fielding side. This is achieved in four ways: [no-ball](/wiki/No-ball "No-ball"), a penalty of one extra conceded by the bowler if they break the rules (often by failing to bowl the ball before their front foot passes the popping crease at their end);[[103]](#cite_note-Law24-105) [wide](/wiki/Wide_(cricket) "Wide (cricket)"), a penalty of one extra conceded by the bowler if they bowl so that the ball is out of the batter's reach;[[104]](#cite_note-Law25a-106) [bye](/wiki/Bye_(cricket) "Bye (cricket)"), an extra awarded if the batter misses the ball and it goes past the wicket-keeper and gives the batters time to run in the conventional way;[[105]](#cite_note-Law26-107) and [leg bye](/wiki/Leg_bye "Leg bye"), as for a bye except that the ball has hit the batter's body, though not their bat.[[105]](#cite_note-Law26-107) If the bowler has bowled an illegal delivery (i.e., a no-ball or a wide), the bowler's team incurs an additional penalty because that ball (i.e., delivery) has to be bowled again, and hence the batting side has the opportunity to score more runs from this extra ball. In addition, the ways in which the batters can be dismissed on an illegal delivery greatly narrow down; in the case of a no-ball, which is the more egregious type of illegal delivery, the only common way in which the batters can be dismissed is by being run out.[[103]](#cite_note-Law24-105)[[104]](#cite_note-Law25a-106)

#### Dismissals

Main article: [Dismissal (cricket)](/wiki/Dismissal_(cricket) "Dismissal (cricket)")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b1/Cricket_Umpire_dismissal.jpg/250px-Cricket_Umpire_dismissal.jpg)](/wiki/File:Cricket_Umpire_dismissal.jpg)

The umpire signaling a dismissal

There are nine ways in which a batter can be dismissed: five relatively common and four extremely rare. When a batter is dismissed, they are said to have 'lost their wicket', and are barred from batting again in that inning; their team is also said to have 'lost a wicket'. Once a team has lost 10 wickets, its innings is over.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/66/Cricketer_bowled.jpg/250px-Cricketer_bowled.jpg)](/wiki/File:Cricketer_bowled.jpg)

Most common dismissals involve the wickets, such as when the ball is bowled at the striker's wicket.

The common forms of dismissal are:

* [bowled](/wiki/Bowled "Bowled") – when the striker fails to prevent a delivery from hitting their wicket[[106]](#cite_note-Law30-108)
* [caught](/wiki/Caught "Caught") – when a ball struck by the bat is caught by a fielder before it hits the ground[[107]](#cite_note-Law32-109)
* [leg before wicket](/wiki/Leg_before_wicket "Leg before wicket") – when the striker's body 'unfairly' prevents a delivery from hitting the wicket[[108]](#cite_note-Law36-110)
* [run out](/wiki/Run_out "Run out") – generally when the ball is thrown at a wicket by a fielder while the batters are running between the wickets[[109]](#cite_note-Law38-111)
* [stumped](/wiki/Stumped "Stumped") – a special type of run out involving the wicket-keeper and striker[[110]](#cite_note-Law39-112)

Rare methods are:

* [hit wicket](/wiki/Hit_wicket "Hit wicket") – a striker hitting their own wicket[[111]](#cite_note-Law35-113)
* [hit the ball twice](/wiki/Hit_the_ball_twice "Hit the ball twice")[[112]](#cite_note-Law34-114)
* [obstructing the field](/wiki/Obstructing_the_field "Obstructing the field")[[113]](#cite_note-Law37-115)
* [timed out](/wiki/Timed_out "Timed out") – a batter failing to enter the field in a timely manner[[114]](#cite_note-Law31-116)

The Laws state that the fielding team, usually the bowler in practice, must appeal for a dismissal before the umpire can give their decision. If the batter is out, the umpire raises a forefinger and says "Out!"; otherwise, the umpire will shake their head and say "Not out".[[115]](#cite_note-Law27-117) There is, effectively, a tenth method of dismissal, [retired out](/wiki/Retired_out "Retired out") (self-dismissal – generally permanent except in cases of injury), which is not an on-field dismissal as such but rather a retrospective one for which no fielder is credited.[[116]](#cite_note-Law2RO-118)

#### Bowling

Main article: [Bowling (cricket)](/wiki/Bowling_(cricket) "Bowling (cricket)")
