Most bowlers are considered specialists in that they are selected for the team because of their skill as a bowler, although some are all-rounders, and even specialist batters bowl occasionally. These specialists bowl "spells" that are generally 4 to 8 overs long in order not to physically exhaust the bowler, cause muscle strain and stress the skeleton. The rules prevent a single bowler from bowling consecutive overs, resulting in at least two bowlers alternating each over. If the captain wants a bowler to "change ends", another bowler must temporarily fill in so that the change is not immediate.[[96]](#cite_note-Law22-98) The action of bowling the ball is akin to throwing, with the caveat that a bowler's elbow extension is almost entirely restricted, resulting in most bowlers maintaining a straight arm when releasing the ball during their delivery stride. Additionally, while the bowler is not required to pitch (bounce) the ball, a full toss (non-bouncing) delivery that reaches the striker above waist height is penalised as a [no-ball](/wiki/No-ball "No-ball").

A bowler reaches their delivery stride by means of a "run-up", and an over is deemed to have begun when the bowler starts their run-up for the first delivery of that over, the ball then being "in play".[[96]](#cite_note-Law22-98) Fast bowlers, or pacemen, need momentum, taking a lengthy run up, while bowlers with a slow delivery take no more than a couple of steps before bowling. The fastest bowlers can deliver the ball at a speed of over 145 kilometres per hour (90 mph), and they sometimes rely on sheer speed to try to defeat the batter, who is forced to react very quickly.[[117]](#cite_note-pace-119) Other fast bowlers rely on a mixture of speed and guile by making the ball [seam](/wiki/Seam_bowling "Seam bowling") or [swing](/wiki/Swing_bowling "Swing bowling") (i.e., curve) in flight. This type of delivery can deceive a batter into miscuing their shot, for example, so that the ball just touches the edge of the bat and can then be "caught behind" by the wicket-keeper or a slip fielder.[[117]](#cite_note-pace-119) At the other end of the bowling scale is the [spin bowler](/wiki/Spin_bowling "Spin bowling"), who bowls at a relatively slow pace and relies entirely on guile to deceive the batter. A spinner will often "buy their wicket" by "tossing one up" (in a slower, steeper [parabolic](/wiki/Parabolic_trajectory "Parabolic trajectory") path) to lure the batter into making a poor shot. The batter has to be very wary of such deliveries, as the batter is often "flighted" or spun so that the ball will not behave quite as the batter expects it to, and the batter could be "trapped" into getting themself out. Accidental full toss deliveries can also get wickets, as the failure of the ball to bounce can surprise a batsman or induce a poor stroke in an effort to punish the poor delivery with a boundary hit.[[118]](#cite_note-spin-120) In between the pacemen and the spinners are the medium-paced seamers, who rely on persistent accuracy to try to contain the rate of scoring and wear down the batter's concentration.[[117]](#cite_note-pace-119)

#### Specialist roles

Main articles: [Captain (cricket)](/wiki/Captain_(cricket) "Captain (cricket)") and [Wicket-keeper](/wiki/Wicket-keeper "Wicket-keeper")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c9/Southgate_CC_v_Stanmore_CC_at_Walker_Cricket_Ground%2C_Southgate%2C_London_22.jpg/250px-Southgate_CC_v_Stanmore_CC_at_Walker_Cricket_Ground%2C_Southgate%2C_London_22.jpg)](/wiki/File:Southgate_CC_v_Stanmore_CC_at_Walker_Cricket_Ground,_Southgate,_London_22.jpg)

The wicket-keeper has gloves to receive deliveries that the striker fails to hit. He may also elect to wear protection such as leg guards and a helmet

The captain is often the most experienced player in the team, certainly the most tactically astute, and can possess any of the main skillsets as a batter, a bowler or a wicket-keeper. Within the Laws, the captain has certain responsibilities in terms of nominating their players to the umpires before the match and ensuring that the captain's players conduct themselves "within the spirit and traditions of the game as well as within the Laws".[[76]](#cite_note-Law1-77)

The wicket-keeper (sometimes called simply the "keeper") is a specialist fielder subject to various rules within the Laws about their equipment and demeanour. The wicket-keeper is the only member of the fielding side who can effect a [stumping](/wiki/Stumped "Stumped") and is the only one permitted to wear gloves and external leg guards.[[119]](#cite_note-Law40-121)

Depending on their primary skills, the other ten players in the team tend to be classified as specialist batters or specialist bowlers. Generally, a team will include five or six specialist batters, and four or five specialist bowlers, plus the wicket-keeper.[[120]](#cite_note-122)[[121]](#cite_note-123)

### Match closure

Main article: [Result (cricket)](/wiki/Result_(cricket) "Result (cricket)")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5f/Southgate_CC_v_Stanmore_CC_at_Walker_Cricket_Ground%2C_Southgate%2C_London_11.jpg/250px-Southgate_CC_v_Stanmore_CC_at_Walker_Cricket_Ground%2C_Southgate%2C_London_11.jpg)](/wiki/File:Southgate_CC_v_Stanmore_CC_at_Walker_Cricket_Ground,_Southgate,_London_11.jpg)

Match results are sometimes determined by a final dismissal, either because the batting team's last wicket falls, or in a limited-overs match, the chance to score is denied on the final delivery.

There are a number of ways that a cricket match can end and its result be described, depending on whether the team batting first or last wins as well as the format of the game.

If the team batting last is 'all out' having scored fewer runs than their opponents, they are said to have "lost by *n* runs" (where *n* is the difference between the aggregate number of runs scored by the teams). If the team that bats last scores enough runs to win, it is said to have "won by *n* wickets", where *n* is the number of wickets left to fall (batters yet to be dismissed) until the team would have been all out. For example, a team that passes its opponents' total having lost six wickets (i.e., six of their batters have been dismissed) wins the match "by four wickets", since the team would only have been prevented from scoring the winning runs if four more of its batters had been dismissed, which would have resulted in all but one of its eleven batters being dismissed.[[86]](#cite_note-result-88)

In a two-innings-a-side match, one team's combined first and second innings total may be less than the other side's first innings total. The team with the greater score is then said to have "won by an innings and *n* runs" and does not need to bat again: *n* is the difference between the two teams' aggregate scores. If the team batting last is all out and both sides have scored the same number of runs, then the match is a [tie](/wiki/Result_(cricket)#Tie "Result (cricket)"); this result is quite rare in matches of two innings a side with only 62 happening in [first-class matches](/wiki/First-class_cricket "First-class cricket") from the earliest known instance in 1741 until January 2017. In the [traditional form](/wiki/Test_cricket "Test cricket") of the game, if the time allotted for the match expires before either side can win, then the game is declared a [draw](/wiki/Result_(cricket)#Draw "Result (cricket)").[[86]](#cite_note-result-88)

If the match has only a single innings per side, then usually a maximum number of overs applies to each innings. Such a match is called a "[limited overs](/wiki/Limited_overs_cricket "Limited overs cricket")" or "one-day" match, and the side scoring more runs wins regardless of the number of wickets lost, so that a draw cannot occur. In some cases, ties are broken by having each team bat for a one-over innings known as a [Super Over](/wiki/Super_Over "Super Over"); subsequent Super Overs may be played if the first Super Over ends in a tie. If this kind of match is temporarily interrupted by bad weather, then a complex mathematical formula, known as the [Duckworth–Lewis–Stern (DLS) method](/wiki/Duckworth%E2%80%93Lewis%E2%80%93Stern_method "Duckworth–Lewis–Stern method") after its developers, is often used to recalculate a new target score. A one-day match can also be declared a "no-result" if fewer than a previously agreed number of overs have been bowled by either team, in circumstances that make normal resumption of play impossible, for example, wet weather.[[86]](#cite_note-result-88)

In all forms of cricket, the umpires can abandon the match if bad light or rain makes it impossible to continue.[[122]](#cite_note-Law3-124) There have been instances of entire matches, even [Test matches](/wiki/Test_cricket "Test cricket") scheduled to be played over five days, being lost to bad weather without a ball being bowled, for example, the third Test of the 1970/71 series in Australia.[[123]](#cite_note-125)

#### Innings

Main article: [Innings](/wiki/Innings "Innings")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6a/Highgate_Cricket_Club_manual_scoreboard_detail_at_Crouch_End%2C_London_1.jpg/250px-Highgate_Cricket_Club_manual_scoreboard_detail_at_Crouch_End%2C_London_1.jpg)](/wiki/File:Highgate_Cricket_Club_manual_scoreboard_detail_at_Crouch_End,_London_1.jpg)

The final innings of the match generally ends when the batting team either scores the winning runs (known as reaching its target score) or runs out of "resources" (either losing 10 wickets or running out of time/overs to bat) and thus can not win.

The innings (ending with 's' in both singular and plural form) is the term used for each phase of play during a match. Depending on the type of match being played, each team has either one or two innings. Sometimes all eleven members of the batting side take a turn to bat but, for various reasons, an innings can end before they have all done so. The innings terminates if the batting team is "all out", a term defined by the Laws: "At the [fall of a wicket](/wiki/Dismissal_(cricket) "Dismissal (cricket)") or the retirement of a batter, further balls remain to be bowled but no further batter is available to come in".[[81]](#cite_note-inns-82) In this situation, one of the batters has not been dismissed and is termed [not out](/wiki/Not_out "Not out"); this is because he has no partners left and there must always be two active batters while the innings is in progress.

An innings may end early while there are still two not out batters:[[81]](#cite_note-inns-82)

* the batting team's [captain](/wiki/Captain_(cricket) "Captain (cricket)") [may declare the innings closed](/wiki/Declaration_and_forfeiture "Declaration and forfeiture"), even though some of the captain's players have not had a turn to bat: this is a tactical decision by the captain, usually because the captain believes that their team have scored sufficient runs and need time to dismiss the opposition in their innings
* the set number of [overs](/wiki/Over_(cricket) "Over (cricket)") (i.e., in a limited overs match) have been bowled
* the match has ended prematurely due to bad weather or running out of time
* in the final innings of the match, the batting side has reached its target (i.e., scored more runs than the opposition) and won the game.

### Umpires and scorers

Main articles: [Umpire (cricket)](/wiki/Umpire_(cricket) "Umpire (cricket)"), [Scoring (cricket)](/wiki/Scoring_(cricket) "Scoring (cricket)"), and [Cricket statistics](/wiki/Cricket_statistics "Cricket statistics")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e9/Cricket_Umpire.jpg/250px-Cricket_Umpire.jpg)](/wiki/File:Cricket_Umpire.jpg)

An umpire signals a decision to the scorers.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/61/Adelaide_Scoreboard1206.jpg/250px-Adelaide_Scoreboard1206.jpg)](/wiki/File:Adelaide_Scoreboard1206.jpg)

The [Adelaide Oval](/wiki/Adelaide_Oval "Adelaide Oval") cricket scoreboard during an [Ashes Test](/wiki/The_Ashes "The Ashes") in Australia

The game on the field is regulated by the two [umpires](/wiki/Umpire_(cricket) "Umpire (cricket)"), one of whom stands behind the wicket at the bowler's end and the other in a position called "square leg", which is about 15–20 m (49–66 ft) away from the batter on strike and in line with the popping crease on which that umpire is taking guard. The umpires have several responsibilities, including adjudication on whether a ball has been correctly bowled (i.e., not a [no-ball](/wiki/No-ball "No-ball") or a [wide](/wiki/Wide_(cricket) "Wide (cricket)")); when a run is scored; whether a batter is out (the fielding side must first [appeal](/wiki/Appeal_(cricket) "Appeal (cricket)") to the umpire, usually with the phrase "How's that?" or "Howzat?"); when intervals start and end; and the suitability of the pitch, field and weather for playing the game. The umpires are authorised to interrupt or even abandon a match due to circumstances likely to endanger the players, such as a damp pitch or deterioration of the light.[[122]](#cite_note-Law3-124)

Off the field in televised matches, there is usually a [third umpire](/wiki/Third_umpire "Third umpire") who can make decisions on certain incidents with the aid of video evidence. The third umpire is mandatory under the playing conditions for [Test](/wiki/Test_cricket "Test cricket") and [Limited Overs International](/wiki/Limited_Overs_International "Limited Overs International") matches played between two ICC full member countries. These matches also have a [match referee](/wiki/Match_referee "Match referee") whose job is to ensure that play is within the Laws and the spirit of the game.[[122]](#cite_note-Law3-124)

The match details, including runs and dismissals, are recorded by two official [scorers](/wiki/Scoring_(cricket) "Scoring (cricket)"), one representing each team. The scorers are directed by the hand signals of an umpire (see image, right). For example, the umpire raises a forefinger to signal that the batter is out (has been dismissed); the umpire raises both arms above their head if the batter has hit the ball for six runs. The scorers are required by the Laws to record all runs scored, wickets taken, and overs bowled; in practice, they also note significant amounts of additional data relating to the game.[[124]](#cite_note-126)

A match's [statistics](/wiki/Cricket_statistics "Cricket statistics") are summarised on a [scorecard](/wiki/Cricket_scorecard "Cricket scorecard"). Prior to the popularisation of scorecards, most scoring was done by men sitting on vantage points cuttings notches on [tally sticks](/wiki/Tally_stick "Tally stick"), and runs were originally called notches.[[125]](#cite_note-127) According to historian [Rowland Bowen](/wiki/Rowland_Bowen "Rowland Bowen"), the earliest known scorecard templates were introduced in 1776 by T. Pratt of [Sevenoaks](/wiki/Sevenoaks "Sevenoaks") and soon came into general use.[[126]](#cite_note-128) It is believed that scorecards were printed and sold at [Lord's](/wiki/Lord%27s "Lord's") for the first time in 1846.[[127]](#cite_note-129)

Scores are displayed differently depending on location, although it is standard to show how many wickets have been lost and how many runs a team has made. Within Australia, the format is Wickets/Runs, while in the rest of the world, the format is Runs/Wickets. For example, a score of 125 runs with 4 wickets lost would be displayed as 4/125 or 125/4, respectively.[[128]](#cite_note-130)

### Spirit of the Game

Main article: [Laws of Cricket](/wiki/Laws_of_Cricket "Laws of Cricket")

Besides observing the Laws, cricketers must respect the "Spirit of Cricket", a concept encompassing [sportsmanship](/wiki/Sportsmanship "Sportsmanship"), fair play and mutual respect. This spirit has long been considered an integral part of the sport but is only nebulously defined. Amidst concern that the spirit was weakening, in 2000, a Preamble was added to the Laws instructing all participants to play within the spirit of the game. The Preamble was last updated in 2017, now opening with the line:[[129]](#cite_note-preamble-131)

> Cricket owes much of its appeal and enjoyment to the fact that it should be played not only according to the Laws, but also within the Spirit of Cricket.

The Preamble is a short statement intended to emphasise the "positive behaviours that make cricket an exciting game that encourages leadership, friendship, and teamwork".[[130]](#cite_note-2017_changes-132) Its second line states that, "the major responsibility for ensuring fair play rests with the captains, but extends to all players, match officials and, especially in [junior cricket](/wiki/Youth_cricket "Youth cricket"), teachers, coaches and parents".[[129]](#cite_note-preamble-131)

The umpires are the sole judges of fair and unfair play. They are required under the Laws to intervene in case of dangerous or unfair play or in cases of unacceptable conduct by a player.

Previous versions of the Spirit identified actions that were deemed contrary (for example, appealing knowing that the batter is not out), but all specifics are now covered in the *Laws of Cricket*, the relevant governing playing regulations and disciplinary codes, or left to the judgement of the umpires, captains, their clubs and governing bodies. The terse expression of the Spirit of Cricket now avoids trying to enumerate the diverse cultural conventions that exist in the detail of sportsmanship, or its absence.[*[clarification needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*]

## Women's cricket

Main article: [Women's cricket](/wiki/Women%27s_cricket "Women's cricket")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/62/Mithali_Raj_Truro_2012.jpg/250px-Mithali_Raj_Truro_2012.jpg)](/wiki/File:Mithali_Raj_Truro_2012.jpg)

[Mithali Raj](/wiki/Mithali_Raj "Mithali Raj") of India is the highest run scorer in women's international cricket.

Women's cricket was first recorded in [Surrey](/wiki/Surrey "Surrey") in 1745.[[131]](#cite_note-133) International development began at the start of the 20th century, and the first Test match was played between [Australia](/wiki/Australia_Women_cricket_team "Australia Women cricket team") and [England](/wiki/England_women%27s_cricket_team "England women's cricket team") in December 1934.[[132]](#cite_note-ICC20C-134) The following year, [New Zealand](/wiki/New_Zealand_Womens_cricket_team "New Zealand Womens cricket team") joined them, and in 2007 [Netherland](/wiki/Netherlands_women_cricket_team "Netherlands women cricket team") became the tenth women's Test nation when they made their debut against [South Africa](/wiki/South_Africa_women_cricket_team "South Africa women cricket team"). In 1958, the International Women's Cricket Council was founded (it merged with the ICC in 2005).[[132]](#cite_note-ICC20C-134) In 1973, the first Cricket World Cup of any kind took place when a Women's World Cup was held in England.[[132]](#cite_note-ICC20C-134) In 2005, the [International Women's Cricket Council](/wiki/International_Women%27s_Cricket_Council "International Women's Cricket Council") was merged with the International Cricket Council (ICC) to form one unified body to help manage and develop cricket. The ICC Women's Rankings were launched on 1 October 2015 covering all three formats of women's cricket. In October 2018 following the ICC's decision to award T20 International status to all members, the Women's rankings were split into separate [ODI](/wiki/One_Day_International "One Day International") (for Full Members) and T20I lists.[[133]](#cite_note-135)

## Governance

Main article: [International Cricket Council](/wiki/International_Cricket_Council "International Cricket Council")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/48/International_Cricket_Council_members_%28by_status%29_Current.svg/500px-International_Cricket_Council_members_%28by_status%29_Current.svg.png)](/wiki/File:International_Cricket_Council_members_(by_status)_Current.svg)

[ICC](/wiki/International_Cricket_Council "International Cricket Council") member nations. The (highest level) Test playing nations are shown in red; the associate member nations are shown in orange, with those with [ODI status](/wiki/One_Day_International "One Day International") in a darker shade; suspended or former members are shown in dark grey.

The [International Cricket Council](/wiki/International_Cricket_Council "International Cricket Council") (ICC), which has its headquarters in [Dubai](/wiki/Dubai "Dubai"), is the global governing body of cricket. It was founded as the Imperial Cricket Conference in 1909 by representatives from England, Australia and South Africa, renamed the International Cricket Conference in 1965 and took up its current name in 1989.[[132]](#cite_note-ICC20C-134) The ICC in 2017 has 105 member nations, twelve of which hold full membership and can play [Test cricket](/wiki/Test_cricket "Test cricket").[[134]](#cite_note-136) The ICC is responsible for the organisation and governance of cricket's major international tournaments, notably the men's and women's versions of the [Cricket World Cup](/wiki/Cricket_World_Cup "Cricket World Cup"). It also appoints the umpires and referees that officiate at all sanctioned Test matches, Limited Overs Internationals and Twenty20 Internationals.

Each member nation has a national cricket board which regulates cricket matches played in its country, selects the national squad, and organises home and away tours for the national team.[[135]](#cite_note-137) In the West Indies, which for cricket purposes is a federation of nations, these matters are addressed by [Cricket West Indies](/wiki/Cricket_West_Indies "Cricket West Indies").[[136]](#cite_note-138)

## Forms of cricket

Main article: [Forms of cricket](/wiki/Forms_of_cricket "Forms of cricket")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/13/England_vs_South_Africa.jpg/250px-England_vs_South_Africa.jpg)](/wiki/File:England_vs_South_Africa.jpg)

A [Test match](/wiki/Test_cricket "Test cricket") between South Africa and England in January 2005. The men wearing black trousers are the [umpires](/wiki/Umpire_(cricket) "Umpire (cricket)"). Teams in Test cricket, [first-class cricket](/wiki/First-class_cricket "First-class cricket") and [club cricket](/wiki/Club_cricket "Club cricket") wear traditional white uniforms and use red [cricket balls](/wiki/Cricket_ball "Cricket ball").

Cricket is a multifaceted sport with multiple formats that can effectively be divided into [first-class cricket](/wiki/First-class_cricket "First-class cricket"), [limited overs cricket](/wiki/Limited_overs_cricket "Limited overs cricket"), and historically, [single wicket cricket](/wiki/Single_wicket_cricket "Single wicket cricket").

The highest standard is [Test cricket](/wiki/Test_cricket "Test cricket") (always written with a capital "T") which is in effect the international version of first-class cricket and is restricted to teams representing the twelve countries that are full members of the ICC (see above). Although the term "Test match" was not coined until much later, Test cricket is deemed to have begun with two matches between [Australia](/wiki/Australia_national_cricket_team "Australia national cricket team") and [England](/wiki/England_national_cricket_team "England national cricket team") in the [1876–77 Australian season](/wiki/1876-77_Australian_cricket_season "1876-77 Australian cricket season"); since 1882, most Test series between England and Australia have been played for a trophy known as [The Ashes](/wiki/The_Ashes "The Ashes"). The term "first-class", in general usage, is applied to top-level domestic cricket. Test matches are played over five days and first-class over three to four days; in all of these matches, the teams are allotted two innings each and the [draw](/wiki/Draw_(cricket) "Draw (cricket)") is a valid result.[[139]](#cite_note-141)

Limited overs cricket is always scheduled for completion in a single day, and the teams are allotted one innings each. There are two main types: [List A](/wiki/List_A_cricket "List A cricket") which normally allows fifty overs per team; and [Twenty20](/wiki/Twenty20 "Twenty20") in which the teams have twenty overs each. Both of the limited overs forms are played internationally as [Limited Overs Internationals](/wiki/Limited_Overs_International "Limited Overs International") (LOI) and [Twenty20 Internationals](/wiki/Twenty20_International "Twenty20 International") (T20I). List A was introduced in England in the 1963 season as a knockout cup contested by the first-class county clubs. In 1969, a national league competition was established. The concept was gradually introduced to the other leading cricket countries and the first limited overs international was played in 1971. In 1975, the first [Cricket World Cup](/wiki/Cricket_World_Cup "Cricket World Cup") took place in England. Twenty20 is a new variant of limited overs itself with the purpose being to complete the match within about three to four hours, usually in an evening session. The first [Twenty20 World Championship](/wiki/Twenty20_World_Championship "Twenty20 World Championship") was held in 2007. In addition, a few full-member cricket boards have decided to start leagues that are played in the [T10 format](/wiki/T10_cricket "T10 cricket"),[[140]](#cite_note-142)[[141]](#cite_note-143)[[142]](#cite_note-144)[[143]](#cite_note-145) in which games are intended to last approximately 90 minutes.[[144]](#cite_note-146)[[145]](#cite_note-147) Most recently, in 2021, the England and Wales Cricket Board (ECB) introduced a new league featuring a hundred-ball tournament, known as [The Hundred](/wiki/The_Hundred_(cricket) "The Hundred (cricket)").[[146]](#cite_note-148) Limited overs matches cannot be drawn, although a [tie](/wiki/Tie_(cricket) "Tie (cricket)") is possible and an unfinished match is a "[no result](/wiki/No-result "No-result")".[[147]](#cite_note-149)[[148]](#cite_note-150)

Single wicket was popular in the 18th and 19th centuries, and its matches were generally considered top-class. In this form, although each team may have from one to six players, there is only one batter in at a time, and that batter must face every delivery bowled while their innings lasts. Single wicket has rarely been played since limited overs cricket began. Matches tended to have two innings per team like a full first-class one and they could end in a draw.[[149]](#cite_note-151)

## Competitions

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/44/Cricket_in_Kerava_IMG_8904_C.JPG/250px-Cricket_in_Kerava_IMG_8904_C.JPG)](/wiki/File:Cricket_in_Kerava_IMG_8904_C.JPG)

Cricket match in [Kerava, Finland](/wiki/Kerava "Kerava"), 2011

Cricket is played at both the international and domestic level. There is one major international championship per format, and top-level domestic competitions mirror the three main international formats. There are now a number of [T20 leagues](/wiki/T20_leagues "T20 leagues"), which have spawned a "T20 freelancer" phenomenon.[[150]](#cite_note-152)

### International competitions

Main article: [International cricket](/wiki/International_cricket "International cricket")

Most international matches are played as parts of 'tours', when one nation travels to another for a number of weeks or months, and plays a number of matches of various sorts against the host nation. Sometimes a [perpetual trophy](/wiki/Test_cricket#Perpetual_trophies "Test cricket") is awarded to the winner of the Test series, the most famous of which is [The Ashes](/wiki/The_Ashes "The Ashes").

The ICC also organises competitions that are for several countries at once, including the [ICC Men's Cricket World Cup](/wiki/ICC_Men%27s_Cricket_World_Cup "ICC Men's Cricket World Cup"), [ICC T20 World Cup](/wiki/ICC_T20_World_Cup "ICC T20 World Cup") and [ICC Champions Trophy](/wiki/ICC_Champions_Trophy "ICC Champions Trophy"). A league competition for Test matches played as part of normal tours, the [ICC World Test Championship](/wiki/World_Test_Championship "World Test Championship"), had been proposed several times, and its [first instance](/wiki/2019%E2%80%9321_ICC_World_Test_Championship "2019–21 ICC World Test Championship") began in 2019. A league competition for [ODIs](/wiki/One_Day_International "One Day International"), the [ICC Cricket World Cup Super League](/wiki/ICC_Cricket_World_Cup_Super_League "ICC Cricket World Cup Super League"), began in August 2020 and lasted only for one edition. The ICC maintains [Test rankings](/wiki/ICC_Test_Championship "ICC Test Championship"), [ODI rankings](/wiki/ICC_ODI_Championship "ICC ODI Championship") and [T20 rankings](/wiki/ICC_T20I_Championship "ICC T20I Championship") systems for the countries which play these forms of cricket.

Competitions for member nations of the ICC with [Associate status](/wiki/List_of_International_Cricket_Council_members#Associate_members "List of International Cricket Council members") include the [ICC Intercontinental Cup](/wiki/ICC_Intercontinental_Cup "ICC Intercontinental Cup"), for first-class cricket matches, and the [World Cricket League](/wiki/World_Cricket_League "World Cricket League") for one-day matches, the final matches of which now also serve as the [ICC World Cup Qualifier](/wiki/ICC_World_Cup_Qualifier "ICC World Cup Qualifier").

The game's only appearance in an [Olympic Games](/wiki/Olympic_Games "Olympic Games") was the [1900 Olympics](/wiki/Cricket_at_the_1900_Summer_Olympics "Cricket at the 1900 Summer Olympics").[[151]](#cite_note-153) However, it is scheduled to make a return, with the [T20](/wiki/Twenty20 "Twenty20") format of the game, in the [2028 Summer Olympics](/wiki/2028_Summer_Olympics "2028 Summer Olympics") in Los Angeles.[[152]](#cite_note-154)[[153]](#cite_note-155)

#### First-class

Main article: [List of current first-class cricket teams](/wiki/List_of_current_first-class_cricket_teams "List of current first-class cricket teams")

First-class cricket in England is played for the most part by the 18 county clubs which contest the [County Championship](/wiki/County_Championship "County Championship"). The concept of a [champion county](/wiki/Champion_County "Champion County") has existed since the 18th century but the official competition was not established until 1890.[[48]](#cite_note-AMCS-49) The most successful club has been [Yorkshire](/wiki/Yorkshire_County_Cricket_Club "Yorkshire County Cricket Club"), who had won 32 official titles (plus one shared) as of 2019.[[154]](#cite_note-156)

Australia established its national first-class championship in 1892–93 when the [Sheffield Shield](/wiki/Sheffield_Shield "Sheffield Shield") was introduced. In Australia, the first-class teams represent the various states.[[155]](#cite_note-CH175-157) [New South Wales](/wiki/New_South_Wales_cricket_team "New South Wales cricket team") has the highest number of titles.

The other ICC full members have national championship trophies called the [Ahmad Shah Abdali 4-day Tournament](/wiki/Ahmad_Shah_Abdali_4-day_Tournament "Ahmad Shah Abdali 4-day Tournament") (Afghanistan); the [National Cricket League](/wiki/National_Cricket_League "National Cricket League") (Bangladesh); the [Ranji Trophy](/wiki/Ranji_Trophy "Ranji Trophy") (India); the [Inter-Provincial Championship](/wiki/Inter-Provincial_Championship "Inter-Provincial Championship") (Ireland); the [Plunket Shield](/wiki/Plunket_Shield "Plunket Shield") (New Zealand); the [Quaid-e-Azam Trophy](/wiki/Quaid-e-Azam_Trophy "Quaid-e-Azam Trophy") (Pakistan); the [Currie Cup](/wiki/Sunfoil_Series "Sunfoil Series") (South Africa); the [Premier Trophy](/wiki/Premier_Trophy "Premier Trophy") (Sri Lanka); the [Shell Shield](/wiki/Regional_Four_Day_Competition "Regional Four Day Competition") (West Indies); and the [Logan Cup](/wiki/Logan_Cup "Logan Cup") (Zimbabwe).

### Club and school cricket

Main articles: [Village cricket](/wiki/Village_cricket "Village cricket"), [Club cricket](/wiki/Club_cricket "Club cricket"), and [Schools cricket](/wiki/Schools_cricket "Schools cricket")

The world's earliest known cricket match was a [village cricket](/wiki/Village_cricket "Village cricket") meeting in [Kent](/wiki/Kent "Kent") which has been deduced from a 1640 court case recording a "cricketing" of "the Weald and the Upland" versus "the Chalk Hill" at [Chevening](/wiki/Chevening "Chevening") "about thirty years since" (i.e., c. 1611). Inter-parish contests became popular in the first half of the 17th century and continued to develop through the 18th with the first local leagues being founded in the second half of the 19th.[[23]](#cite_note-DU4-23)

At the grassroots level, local [club cricket](/wiki/Club_cricket "Club cricket") is essentially an amateur pastime for those involved but still usually involves teams playing in competitions at weekends or in the evening. [Schools cricket](/wiki/Schools_cricket "Schools cricket"), first known in southern England in the 17th century, has a similar scenario and both are widely played in the countries where cricket is popular.[[156]](#cite_note-158) Although there can be variations in game format, compared with professional cricket, the Laws are always observed and club/school matches are therefore formal and competitive events.[[157]](#cite_note-159) The sport has numerous informal variants such as [French cricket](/wiki/French_cricket "French cricket").[[158]](#cite_note-160)

## Culture

Main page: [Category:Cricket culture](/wiki/Category:Cricket_culture "Category:Cricket culture")

### Influence on everyday life

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/07/Street_Cricket_in_India_%28edited%29.jpg/250px-Street_Cricket_in_India_%28edited%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Street_Cricket_in_India_(edited).jpg)

[Street cricket](/wiki/Street_cricket "Street cricket") being played in a village in the Himalayas of India

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d3/Jugando_la_Plaquita.JPG/250px-Jugando_la_Plaquita.JPG)](/wiki/File:Jugando_la_Plaquita.JPG)

[Plaquita](/wiki/Plaquita "Plaquita"), a game similar to cricket, being played in the Dominican Republic

Cricket has had a broad impact on popular culture, both in the [Commonwealth of Nations](/wiki/Commonwealth_of_Nations "Commonwealth of Nations") and elsewhere. It has, for example, influenced the [lexicon](/wiki/Lexicon "Lexicon") of these nations, especially the English language, with various phrases such as "that's not cricket" (that's unfair), "had a good [innings](/wiki/Innings#Usage_outside_of_cricket "Innings")" (lived a long life), and "[sticky wicket](/wiki/Sticky_wicket "Sticky wicket")". "On a sticky wicket" (*aka* "sticky dog" or "glue pot")[[159]](#cite_note-161) is a [metaphor](/wiki/Metaphor "Metaphor")[[160]](#cite_note-CalliesKeller2011-162) used to describe a difficult circumstance. It originated as a term for difficult batting conditions in cricket, caused by a damp and soft pitch.[[161]](#cite_note-Hendrickson2001-163)

### In the arts and popular culture

See also: [Cricket in fiction](/wiki/Cricket_in_fiction "Cricket in fiction"), [Cricket in film and television](/wiki/Cricket_in_film_and_television "Cricket in film and television"), and [Cricket poetry](/wiki/Cricket_poetry "Cricket poetry")

Cricket is the subject of works by noted English poets, including [William Blake](/wiki/William_Blake "William Blake") and [Lord Byron](/wiki/Lord_Byron "Lord Byron").[[162]](#cite_note-art-164) *[Beyond a Boundary](/wiki/Beyond_a_Boundary "Beyond a Boundary")* (1963), written by Trinidadian [C. L. R. James](/wiki/C._L._R._James "C. L. R. James"), is often named the best book on any sport ever written.[[163]](#cite_note-165)

In the visual arts, notable cricket paintings include [Albert Chevallier Tayler](/wiki/Albert_Chevallier_Tayler "Albert Chevallier Tayler")'s *[Kent vs Lancashire at Canterbury](/wiki/Kent_vs_Lancashire_at_Canterbury "Kent vs Lancashire at Canterbury")* (1907) and [Russell Drysdale](/wiki/Russell_Drysdale "Russell Drysdale")'s *[The cricketers](/wiki/The_cricketers_(Drysdale) "The cricketers (Drysdale)")* (1948), which has been called "possibly the most famous Australian painting of the 20th century."[[164]](#cite_note-Meacham-166) French [impressionist](/wiki/Impressionism "Impressionism") [Camille Pissarro](/wiki/Camille_Pissarro "Camille Pissarro") painted cricket on a visit to England in the 1890s.[[162]](#cite_note-art-164) [Francis Bacon](/wiki/Francis_Bacon_(artist) "Francis Bacon (artist)"), an avid cricket fan, captured a batter in motion.[[162]](#cite_note-art-164) [Caribbean](/wiki/Caribbean "Caribbean") artist [Wendy Nanan](/wiki/Wendy_Nanan "Wendy Nanan")'s cricket images[[165]](#cite_note-167) are featured in a limited edition first day cover for [Royal Mail](/wiki/Royal_Mail "Royal Mail")'s "World of Invention" stamp issue, which celebrated the London Cricket Conference 1–3 March 2007, first international workshop of its kind and part of the celebrations leading up to the 2007 [Cricket World Cup](/wiki/Cricket_World_Cup "Cricket World Cup").[[166]](#cite_note-168)

In music, many [calypsos](/wiki/Calypso_music "Calypso music") make reference to the Sport of Cricket. See [List of calypso songs about cricket](/wiki/List_of_calypso_songs_about_cricket "List of calypso songs about cricket").

### Influence on other sports

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/66/William_Handcock_Tom_Wills.jpg/250px-William_Handcock_Tom_Wills.jpg)](/wiki/File:William_Handcock_Tom_Wills.jpg)

[Tom Wills](/wiki/Tom_Wills "Tom Wills"), cricketer and co-founder of [Australian football](/wiki/Australian_football "Australian football")

Cricket has close historical ties with [Australian rules football](/wiki/Australian_rules_football "Australian rules football") and many players have [competed at top levels in both sports](/wiki/List_of_Australian_rules_footballers_and_cricketers "List of Australian rules footballers and cricketers").[[167]](#cite_note-169) In 1858, prominent Australian cricketer [Tom Wills](/wiki/Tom_Wills "Tom Wills") called for the formation of a "foot-ball club" with "a code of laws" to keep cricketers fit during the off-season. The [Melbourne Football Club](/wiki/Melbourne_Football_Club "Melbourne Football Club") was founded the following year, and Wills and three other members codified the first laws of the game.[[168]](#cite_note-170) It is typically played on [modified cricket fields](/wiki/Australian_rules_football_playing_field "Australian rules football playing field").[[169]](#cite_note-171)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cc/1876boxscore.jpg/250px-1876boxscore.jpg)](/wiki/File:1876boxscore.jpg)

An 1876 baseball box score

In England, a number of [association football](/wiki/Association_football "Association football") clubs owe their origins to cricketers who sought to play football as a means of keeping fit during the winter months. [Derby County](/wiki/Derby_County_F._C. "Derby County F. C.") was founded as a branch of the [Derbyshire County Cricket Club](/wiki/Derbyshire_County_Cricket_Club "Derbyshire County Cricket Club") in 1884;[[170]](#cite_note-172) [Aston Villa](/wiki/Aston_Villa_F._C. "Aston Villa F. C.") (1874) and [Everton](/wiki/Everton_F._C. "Everton F. C.") (1876) were both founded by members of church cricket teams.[[171]](#cite_note-173) [Sheffield United](/wiki/Sheffield_United_F._C. "Sheffield United F. C.")'s [Bramall Lane](/wiki/Bramall_Lane "Bramall Lane") ground was, from 1854, the home of the [Sheffield Cricket Club](/wiki/Sheffield_Cricket_Club "Sheffield Cricket Club"), and then of [Yorkshire](/wiki/Yorkshire_County_Cricket_Club "Yorkshire County Cricket Club"); it was not used for football until 1862 and was shared by Yorkshire and Sheffield United from 1889 to 1973.[[172]](#cite_note-174)

In the late 19th century, a former cricketer, English-born [Henry Chadwick](/wiki/Henry_Chadwick_(writer) "Henry Chadwick (writer)") of [Brooklyn](/wiki/Brooklyn "Brooklyn"), [New York](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City"), was credited with devising the baseball [box score](/wiki/Box_score_(baseball) "Box score (baseball)")[[173]](#cite_note-175) (which he adapted from the cricket scorecard) for reporting game events. The first box score appeared in an 1859 issue of the *[Clipper](/wiki/New_York_Clipper "New York Clipper")*.[[174]](#cite_note-NPR-176) The statistical record is so central to the game's "historical essence" that Chadwick is sometimes referred to as "the Father of Baseball" because he facilitated the popularity of the sport in its early days.[[175]](#cite_note-Myth-177)

The influence of cricket and other [English sports in British India](/wiki/Sport_in_British_India "Sport in British India") resulted in the [local sports](/wiki/Traditional_games_of_South_Asia "Traditional games of South Asia") becoming standardised by the 1920s.[[176]](#cite_note-178) The 21st century success of the [Indian Premier League](/wiki/Indian_Premier_League "Indian Premier League") also inspired the growth of other sports leagues in India,[[177]](#cite_note-179) with some of the native sports being further modernised, as with the popular [Pro Kabaddi League](/wiki/Pro_Kabaddi_League "Pro Kabaddi League").[[178]](#cite_note-180)

Many amateurs in [first-class cricket](/wiki/First-class_cricket "First-class cricket") were full-time players during the cricket season. Some of the game's greatest players, including [W. G. Grace](/wiki/W._G._Grace "W. G. Grace"), held amateur status.

1. **[^](#cite_ref-87)** The exception to this is if a batter has any type of illness or injury restricting their ability to run; in this case, the batter is allowed a [runner](/wiki/Runner_(cricket) "Runner (cricket)") who can run between the wickets when the batter hits a scoring run or runs,[[84]](#cite_note-Law25-85) though this does not apply in international cricket.[[85]](#cite_note-ICCTestPlayCond-86)
